revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Zamagami]][[ja:座摩神]][[zh:坐摩巫祭神]] {{Infobox deity}}
{{distinguish|{{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}}}} [[File:Jingikan_map.svg|right|thumb|250x250px|{{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} layout The northern office where the {{ill|Ikasuri no Kami|ja|座摩神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|2=ja|3=座摩神|4=zh|5=坐摩巫祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} are enshrined is prominent in the northwest. The {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} is located just to the southwest and is the westernmost major building]] '''Ikasuri no Kami''' (坐摩神, also read as Zamagami) are five deities collectively worshipped in the western precinct of the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}}:
* '''Ikui no Kami''' (生井神) * '''Sakui no Kami''' (福井神) - Also referred to as "栄井神"{{Sfn|Ikasuri no Kami (National Histories)}}. * '''Tsunagai no Kami''' (綱長井神) * '''Hahiki no Kami''' (波比祇神) - Also referred to as "婆比支神"{{Sfn|Ikasuri no Kami (National Histories)}}. * '''Asuha no Kami''' (阿須波神)
The {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}} and {{ill|Ikasuri no Kami|ja|座摩神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|2=ja|3=座摩神|4=zh|5=坐摩巫祭神|1=Ikasuri no Kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|13=User:Immanuelle/Ikasuri no Kami|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} were both prominently worshipped in the Imperial palace. But the Hasshinden were seen as having a cosmological importance<ref>https://d-museum.kokugakuin.ac.jp/eos/detail/?id=8587</ref> and the Ikasuri domestic.<ref>https://en.osaka-info.jp/spot/ikasurijinja/</ref>
== Overview == During the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, these deities were enshrined at the {{ill|Department of Divinities|en|Department of Divinities|qid=Q2264720|lt=Department of Divinities|1=Department of Divinities}} in the western precinct of the {{ill|Heian Palace|ja|平安宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Heian Palace|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 大内裏|1=Heian Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q304501}}. They were served by female priestesses known as {{ill|Mikannagi|ja|御巫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巫|1=Wu (shaman)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3687892}}, specifically the ''Ōmikannagi'' (大御巫, "Great Mikannagi"), ''Ikasuri Mikannagi'' (座摩巫), and others{{Sfn|Mikannagi (National Histories)}}.
The term ''Ikasuri'' is believed to originate from "Ikashiri" (居処領, "domain of residence") or "Ikashiri" (居所知){{Sfn|Ikasuri Mikannagi Gosa (Shikinaisha)|1979}}. These deities were considered guardians of the imperial palace{{Sfn|Ikasuri Mikannagi Gosa (Shikinaisha)|1979}}. Ikui no Kami, Sakui no Kami, and Tsunagai no Kami are deities of wells, symbolizing the palace's abundance as reflected in the ''Manyo-shu'''s "Fujihara Palace Mikui Song"{{SFn|Ancient History of Shinto Shrines|20pp|pp=187-188}}. Meanwhile, Hahiki no Kami and Asuha no Kami are less well defined but are described in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} as children of {{ill|Toshigami|lt=Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大年神|1=Toshigami|lt=Ōtoshi-no-kami|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toshigami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 年神}} and {{ill|Ame no Wakaharu Mizuhime|ja|天和迦流美豆比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ame no Wakaharu Mizuhime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, functioning as guardians of palace grounds{{SFn|Ancient History of Shinto Shrines|20pp|pp=187-188}}.
The ''Department of Divinities'''s northern office featured shrines oriented southward for lesser deities such as the Ikasuri no Kami, while the ''Ōmikannagi'''s principal eight deities were enshrined in the east-facing {{ill|Yatsukami Hall|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden|1=Hasshinden|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yatsukami Hall|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}}. The ''Engishiki'' places the Ikasuri no Kami second in importance after the ''Ōmikannagi'''s deities and mentions them in prayers for the {{ill|Kinen-sai|en|Kinen-sai|qid=Q118906710|lt=Kinensai|1=Kinensai}} and {{ill|Rites of June|ja|六月月次祭|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Rites of June|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}{{Sfn|Ikasuri Mikannagi (National Histories)}}.
=== Priestess Selection === Unlike other {{ill|Mikannagi|ja|御巫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mikannagi|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 巫|1=Wu (shaman)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3687892}} roles, which could be filled by commoners, ''Ikasuri Mikannagi'' had to be selected from girls aged seven or older belonging to the aristocratic {{ill|Tsuge no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja|都祁国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuge no Kuni no Miyatsuko|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 闘鶏国造}} lineage, according to the ''Engishiki''{{SFn|Ancient History of Shinto Shrines|20pp|p=192}}.
== History == === Early Mentions === The ''Shoku Nihongi'' mentions ''Ikasuri Mikannagi'' receiving court Rank in {{ill|Tenpyō|en|Tenpyō|qid=Q1199879|lt=Tenpyō|1=Tenpyō}} 9 (737){{Sfn|Ikasuri Mikannagi (National Histories)}}. Additionally, the {{ill|Kogo Shūi|en|Kogo Shūi|qid=Q531555|lt=Kogo Shūi|1=Kogo Shūi}} compiled in {{ill|Datong (disambiguation)|en|Datong (disambiguation)|qid=Q295082|lt=Daido|1=Datong (disambiguation)}} 2 (807) identifies them as "spirits of the great palace land" (大宮地の霊){{Sfn|Ikasuri Mikannagi (National Histories)}}. In {{ill|Zhenguan|en|Zhenguan|qid=Q3577278|lt=Jōgan|1=Zhenguan}} 1 (859), they were raised to {{ill|Junior Fourth Rank|ja|従四位上|1=Junior Fourth Rank|lt=Junior Fourth Rank, Upper Grade|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|13=User:Immanuelle/Junior Fourth Rank|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 従四位}} alongside other palace deities.
=== Decline === By the {{ill|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|en|Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)|qid=Q8140349|lt=Nanboku-chō period|1=Northern and Southern Courts period (disambiguation)}}, the ancient practices of the ''Department of Divinities'''s rituals began to fade. Following the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|qid=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War|1=Ōnin War}}, the ''Ikasuri Mikannagi'' and their associated deities had largely disappeared{{SFn|Ancient History of Shinto Shrines|20pp|pp=199-200}}.
== Associated Shrines == [[File:Fukunaga-jinja (Kyoto) shaden.JPG|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Fukunaga Shrine|ja|福長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukunaga Shrine|qq=}} (Kyoto)]] [[File:Ikasuri-jinja, haiden.jpg|thumb|200px|right|{{ill|Ikasuri Shrine|en|Ikasuri Shrine|qid=Q11425667|lt=Ikasuri Shrine|1=Ikasuri Shrine}} (Osaka)]] Though the original ''Department of Divinities'' rituals ceased during the medieval period, their legacy continues through shrines like {{ill|Fukunaga Shrine|ja|福長神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fukunaga Shrine|qq=}} in Kyoto, believed to enshrine Sakui no Kami and Tsunagai no Kami{{Sfn|Ikasuri Mikannagi Gosa (Shikinaisha)|1979}}.
In Osaka, {{ill|Ikasuri Shrine|en|Ikasuri Shrine|qid=Q11425667|lt=Ikasuri Shrine|1=Ikasuri Shrine}} and {{ill|Ikukunitama Shrine|en|Ikukunitama Shrine|qid=Q11574477|lt=Ikukunitama Shrine|1=Ikukunitama Shrine}} are associated with the ''Ikasuri no Kami'' and related palace deities. Historical records suggest these shrines were linked to the {{ill|Naniwa Palace|ja|難波宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Naniwa Palace|1=Naniwa Palace|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q3608523}} rituals and reflect the court's emphasis on the Naniwa area{{SFn|Ancient History of Shinto Shrines|20pp|pp=190-192}}.
== Notes == {{Reflist}}
== See Also ==
* {{ill|Hasshinden|ja|八神殿|1=Hasshinden|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|13=User:Immanuelle/Hasshinden|qq=}}

''' {{Wikicite|reference=Izumo Yamashita "Ikasuri Mikannagi"|ref={{Harvid|Ikasuri Mikannagi (National Histories)}}}}. ''' {{Wikicite|reference=Yoshikazu Suzuki "Mikannagi"|ref={{Harvid|Mikannagi (National Histories)}}}}. * {{Cite book|和書|editor=式内社研究会編|author=|chapter=Ikasuri Mikannagi Gosa|year=1979|title=Shikinaisha Survey Report Vol. 1|publisher=[[Kōgakkan University]]|page=|isbn=|ref={{Harvid|Ikasuri Mikannagi Gosa (Shikinaisha)|1979}}}} * {{Cite book|和書|editor=|author=Seiji Okada|year=20pp|chapter=Chapter 8: Rituals of the Department of Divinities—West Precinct Deities and Mikannagi Service|title=Revised Ancient History of Shinto Shrines|publisher=[[Gakuseisha]]|isbn=43pp203020|ref={{Harvid|Ancient History of Shinto Shrines|20pp}}}}
